content: Hi, I'm Kit, and I'm a Professional Rock Climber and Guide. I'm feeling really burnt out and I'm not sure if this is the right career for me anymore.
Hi Kit, sorry to hear that you're feeling burnt out. Can you tell me more about what's leading to these feelings? Is it the physical demands of the job, the travel, or something else?

I think it's a combination of everything. I love rock climbing, but the constant travel and physical demands are taking a toll on me. I'm also feeling really frustrated with the business side of things and the pressure to constantly perform.
It sounds like you're feeling overwhelmed and undervalued in your current role. Have you considered exploring other areas of the outdoor industry, or possibly making a career change altogether?

Yeah, I've been thinking about that a lot lately. I've always been interested in urban sketching, and I've even started taking some classes on the side. Maybe I could find a way to use my artistic skills to help others.

That's a great idea! Using your artistic skills to help others is a wonderful way to make a positive impact. What kind of classes have you been taking, and what do you hope to achieve with your urban sketching?

I've been taking classes at a local art school, and I'm really enjoying it. I'd love to start selling my artwork and see where it takes me. I'm thinking of investing in a new set of sketching tools and practicing regularly.

It sounds like you're really committed to pursuing your passion for urban sketching. Investing in new sketching tools and practicing regularly are great steps to take. Have you thought about how you'll balance your urban sketching with your current work as a Professional Rock Climber and Guide?

To be honest, I'm not sure. I'm thinking of scaling back my hours as a Guide and focusing more on my urban sketching. I've got a show coming up on June 10th, and I'm hoping it will be a good opportunity to get my name out there. I'm also planning a trip to Taiwan in August to learn more about the culture and possibly sell some of my artwork.

It's great that you have a show lined up! Scaling back your hours as a Guide could be a good way to free up time and energy to focus on your urban sketching. What's the plan for your trip to Taiwan, and how are you preparing for it?

I'm really excited about the trip! I've been learning Spanish, but I know I'll need to learn some Mandarin as well. I'm planning to spend a few weeks in Taipei and then head to Kaohsiung to explore the city and sell some of my artwork.

That sounds like an amazing adventure! Learning a new language and navigating cultural differences can be difficult, but it's also a great opportunity to grow and learn. What do you hope to achieve with your urban sketching in Taiwan, and how do you think it will be received?

I hope to be able to use my urban sketching to connect with people and showcase the beauty of the city. I'm a bit nervous about how it will be received, but I'm excited to take the risk and see what happens.

It's great that you're taking the risk and pursuing your passion. Remember that art is a universal language, and people will appreciate your efforts to create something beautiful. What's the most challenging part of preparing for your trip to Taiwan, and how are you overcoming those challenges?
I think the most challenging part is learning the language and navigating the cultural differences. But I'm excited to learn and adapt, and I'm sure it will be a valuable experience.

It's great that you're aware of the challenges and are preparing yourself for them. Learning a new language and navigating cultural differences can be difficult, but it's also a great opportunity to grow and learn. What do you think you'll miss most about your life in the US when you're in Taiwan?
